Title: Reducing the Sinapine Levels of Camelina sativa Seeds Through Targeted Genome Editing of REF1

Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: LOC104746994; LOC104767838; REF1_Chr1 sequence cloned in this study
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Sinapate/sinapine branch in seeds; ferulate branch in leaves
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Midstream oxidation step supplying sinapate/ferulate pools
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidizes sinapaldehyde to sinapate and coniferyl aldehyde to ferulate using NADP+
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor pool for sinapoyl conjugates and sinapine; also supplies ferulate-derived conjugates in leaves
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Seed defense compound with bitter taste; antinutritional factor reducing palatability for livestock
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of all three REF1 homoeologues significantly reduces seed sinapine content by about 56% without affecting key seed quality traits
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: CRISPR/Cas9 triple knockout of all three homoeologues; homoeologue-specific sequencing; HPLC-DAD sinapine quantification; qRT-PCR; non-targeted UPLC-ESI-QTOF-MS metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Camelina contains three REF1 homoeologues on chromosomes 1, 15, and 19 that are strongly expressed in developing seeds and leaves. 2. CRISPR/Cas9-mediated inactivation of all three homoeologues lowered seed sinapine to about 44% of wild type, whereas single REF1_Chr15 mutants did not reduce sinapine. 3. Metabolite profiling showed depletion of sinapoyl conjugates in seeds and feruloyl conjugates in leaves, supporting seed-specific oxidation of sinapaldehyde to sinapate and leaf-specific oxidation of coniferyl aldehyde to ferulate. 4. Seed weight, protein, total fatty acid content, fatty acid profile, germination, and seedling establishment were not detectably impaired.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Highest in 3-week developing seeds (embryos) and also strong in leaves; weaker in flowers and young pods; REF1_Chr15 transcripts lower than combined REF1_Chr1/REF1_Chr19
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highest in developing seeds and leaves in Camelina
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Standard climate chamber growth conditions; tissue context (developing seeds versus leaves) strongly affects inferred substrate usage
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key enzyme for sinapine biosynthesis; not sufficient alone for complete sinapine elimination because residual REF1-independent pathway is indicated
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No detectable penalty in seed weight, protein, total fatty acids, fatty acid composition, germination, lipid mobilization, or seedling establishment; possible untested effects on defense/stress resistance remain
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Non-targeted metabolomics by UPLC-ESI-QTOF-MS; supporting dataset Dataset S3 (pbi70450-sup-0002-DatasetS3.xlsx)
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Validated target for reducing antinutritional sinapine in Camelina seed meal; suitable for stacking with low-glucosinolate or altered-oil traits; partial reduction without agronomic penalty makes it useful for quality improvement
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Residual sinapine in triple mutants supports an alternative REF1-independent route, discussed as potentially involving COMT-mediated methylation at the acid level; Camelina differs from Arabidopsis by lacking substantial sinapoyl malate accumulation and by accumulating chlorogenic acid in leaves
